case_name: Cochrum v. Costa Victoria Healthcare, LLC

summary.brief: Cochrum v. Costa Victoria Healthcare, LLC. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: In 2012, Harvey Cohoon was at a restaurant with his niece, plaintiff Donna Cochrum, when he suddenly fell.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Cohoon was diagnosed with colon cancer. The cancer was not considered aggressive, and was at stage 2.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When Cohoon was admitted to Victoria Center, he was malnourished, had a stage 3 pressure ulcer, and had muscle wasting.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On the 20th day, he was observed to have difficulty swallowing thin liquids, and after an evaluation, his diet was changed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Less than 20 minutes after being served dinner, a nurse found him in respiratory arrest.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The paramedics had to remove large pieces of chicken from his throat before intubating him.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He died the following day due to complications from oxygen deprivation to his brain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A jury returned a verdict in favor of Cochrum on all causes of action.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The court subsequently awarded Cochrum over $350,000 in attorney fees pursuant to the elder abuse claim.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The court granted a motion for judgment notwithstanding the verdict (JNOV), finding insufficient evidence of recklessness to support the elder abuse cause of action.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Cochrum presented the testimony of a nursing expert who offered various theories of how Costa Victoria breached its duty of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: She opined Victoria Center was inadequately staffed.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Cochrum’s nursing expert opined there were multiple breaches of the duty of care in connection with the service of Cohoon’s final meal.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Cochrum’s nursing expert went on to opine that Nurse Grimble breached his duty of care in failing to perform the Heimlich maneuver.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: The court acknowledged there was substantial evidence to support the findings that Cohoon was not served a mechanically soft chopped meal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The court concluded defendants’ conduct amounted to negligence, but not recklessness as required to support a claim for elder abuse.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The court reasoned, “the entire episode concerning Mr. Cohoon was over in less than twelve hours, from the change in dietary plan to the choking.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The judgment is affirmed. The parties shall bear their own costs on appeal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Harvey Cohoon was admitted to Victoria Center for recovery from colon cancer treatment. On the 20th day, he was observed to have difficulty swallowing thin liquids.

focused_exam.general: Patient appeared malnourished with muscle wasting

initial_differential: Dysphagia due to neurological or structural cause

reveal.result_summary: Severe swallowing inability with liquids, recommended 'pudding thick' liquids and mechanical-soft diet
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates high risk of aspiration with thin liquids
reveal.decision_impact: Diet change required to prevent aspiration

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted to Victoria Center for recovery

timeline_phases.name: Swallowing Evaluation
timeline_phases.description: Evaluation of swallowing difficulties and diet modification

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with severe dysphagia requiring diet modification
decision_points.prompt: How should the diet change be communicated to ensure patient safety?

options.description: Ensure diet change is documented in both nursing and kitchen records
options.clinical_reasoning: Proper documentation ensures all staff are aware of dietary restrictions

options.description: Document diet change only in nursing records
options.clinical_reasoning: Incomplete communication may lead to dietary errors

actual_action_defendant.description: Diet change was documented only in nursing records

expected_action_court.description: Diet change should have been documented in both nursing and kitchen records
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Ensures comprehensive communication of dietary needs

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to communicate diet change to kitchen led to improper meal being served
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Diet change should have been communicated to all relevant staff
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care requires comprehensive communication of critical dietary changes

patient_outcome.description: Patient died due to complications from oxygen deprivation to the brain

key_findings: Negligence in failing to serve the proper meal
Failure to adequately monitor during meal

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Proper communication would have prevented choking incident

treatments.treatment: Diet modification to 'pudding thick' liquids and mechanical-soft food
treatments.outcome: Not properly communicated, leading to incorrect meal

court_findings.finding: Defendants were negligent in failing to serve the proper meal
